content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

November 1, 1976


Mr. John C. Morrissey
Vice President and General Counsel
Pacific Gas and Electric Company
77 Beale Street
San Francisco, CA 94106

Attention Tom High

Re: Opinion Request
No. 76-086

Dear Mr. Morrissey:

Thank you for your letter of October 11, 1976,
requesting an opinion regarding campaign statements required
to be filed by the Pacific Gas and Electric Company ("PG&E").
Because your letter raises no substantial questions of in-
terpretation under the Political Reform Act, no formal opinion
will be issued. However, we hope that the following informal
comments will be helpful.

You stated that PG&E has made contributions to the
Good Government Fund, a political action committee which
supports only candidates' campaigns, by providing adminis-
trative support and miscellaneous supplies to the Fund.
PG&E also has made contributions and expenditures in connection
with statewide and local ballot measure campaigns. You
asked whether PG&E may disclose these contributions and
expenditures on separate campaign statements filed pursuant
to the appropriate filing schedules set forth in Government
Code Sections 84200 and 84201 (for the contributions to the
Good Government Fund) and Government Code Section 84203 (for
the contributions and expenditures in connection with ballot
measure campaigns).

A committee that is required to file campaign
statements for both candidate and ballot measure campaigns
conducted in connection with the same election may choose
either the candidate schedule or the ballot measure schedule.
Government Code Section 84204. However, the committee must
disclose information relative to both candidates and ballot
measures on each campaign statement and may not file two
separate series of statements. In other words, the committee
may choose either filing schedule that is applicable, but it
must file pursuant to only one schedule and must disclose
all of the required information in the statements filed on
that schedule.


TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTR [illegible] 322-2469 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-2901 • ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

Mr. John C. Morrissey
November 1, 1976
Page Two

With respect to whether the option provided by
Government Code Section 84204 is even available to FG&E, you
should be aware that the Commission has not yet determined
whether a company whose only candidate related contributions
consist of administrative support for a political action
committee is a committee "supporting or opposing a candidate"
within the meaning of Government Code Section 84200. Con-
sequently, it is unclear at this time whether FG&E, by reason
of its support of the Good Government Fund, is required to
file statements according to the schedules set forth in
Government Code Sections 84200 and 84201, and thus can avail
itself of the option provided by Government Code Section
84204. This issue is before the Commission in the opinion
requested by Kenneth Okel, No. 76-042, and you may want to
monitor the progress of that opinion and submit comments
pertinent to it.

In addition, even if PG&E ultimately is deemed to
be supporting candidates by reason of administrative support
rendered to the Good Government Fund, recent amendments to
the Political Reform Act may simplify the company's reporting
obligations and alleviate several of the problems raised in
your opinion request. A copy of A.B. 1336, approved by the
Governor on September 20, 1976, is enclosed and your attention
is directed to Government Code Section 84204.1 and 84204.2.
This amendment will become effective in January, 1977.

Finally, for purposes of campaign disclosure rela-
tive to the November 2, 1976, general election, you will be
deemed to be in substantial compliance with the Act's report-
ing requirements if you file post-election campaign statements
on both schedules according to your present practice. It
will be acceptable to commence compliance with the advice
contained herein after those filings.

If you have any further questions, please contact
Natalie West, an attorney with the Commission staff. If you
wish to appeal the denial of your opinion request, you may
do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:NEW:plh
Enclosure

sections.question: Whether PG&E may disclose contributions and expenditures to the Good Government Fund on separate campaign statements filed pursuant to Government Code Sections 84200, 84201 (for contributions to the Good Government Fund) and Government Code Section 84203 (for contributions and expenditures in connection with ballot measure campaigns), or whether the company must file on a single unified campaign statement.
sections.conclusion: A committee required to file campaign statements for both candidate and ballot measure campaigns conducted in connection with the same election may choose either the candidate schedule or the ballot measure schedule under Government Code Section 84204, but must disclose information relative to both candidates and ballot measures on each campaign statement and may not file two separate series of statements. However, the Commission has not yet determined whether a company whose only candidate-related contributions consist of administrative support for a political action committee is a committee "supporting or opposing a candidate" within Government Code Section 84200, and therefore it is unclear whether PG&E is required to file under the Section 84200/84201 schedules and can use the Section 84204 option. For the November 2, 1976, general election, PG&E may file post-election campaign statements on both schedules according to its present practice without violation.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a corporation file separate campaign statements for contributions to a political action committee supporting candidates versus expenditures for ballot measures, or must all such disclosures be consolidated on a single filing?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declines to issue a formal opinion but provides informal guidance that a committee must choose one filing schedule and disclose all required information on that schedule, though it is unclear whether PG&E's administrative support to a political action committee makes it a "supporting" committee required to file under these rules. PG&E is deemed in substantial compliance for the November 1976 election if it files on both schedules.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers present; content extracted from body text. Document is informal advice letter with conditional guidance. References pending opinion No. 76-042 and recent legislation (A.B. 4336) affecting the inquiry.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether PG&E may disclose contributions and expenditures to the Good Government Fund on separate campaign statements filed pursuant to Government Code Sections 84200, 84201 (for contributions to the Good Government Fund) and Government Code Section 84203 (for contributions and expenditures in connection with ballot measure campaigns), or whether the company must file on a single unified campaign statement.

CONCLUSION: A committee required to file campaign statements for both candidate and ballot measure campaigns conducted in connection with the same election may choose either the candidate schedule or the ballot measure schedule under Government Code Section 84204, but must disclose information relative to both candidates and ballot measures on each campaign statement and may not file two separate series of statements. However, the Commission has not yet determined whether a company whose only candidate-related contributions consist of administrative support for a political action committee is a committee "supporting or opposing a candidate" within Government Code Section 84200, and therefore it is unclear whether PG&E is required to file under the Section 84200/84201 schedules and can use the Section 84204 option. For the November 2, 1976, general election, PG&E may file post-election campaign statements on both schedules according to its present practice without violation.

embedding.summary: The FPPC informally advises Pacific Gas and Electric Company regarding campaign statement filing requirements for its contributions to a political action committee and ballot measure campaigns, declining to issue a formal opinion on whether separate filings may be made and clarifying that any single committee must use only one applicable filing schedule while disclosing all required information.
